perturbation.original_text: Each of the Company and the Investors shall be responsible for its own fees and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation, execution, and effectuation of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, including, but not limited to attorneys' fees incurred in connection with the negotiation and execution of this Agreement and all other activities related to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Company and the Investors shall be responsible for its own fees and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation, execution, and effectuation of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, excluding attorney fees.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding attorney fees, the contract contradicts the general principle and specific statutes in Delaware, where the agreement is governed, regarding the right to seek attorney fees in certain breach of contract cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding attorney's fees in contract disputes
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law generally allows for recovery of attorney's fees in breach of contract cases under certain circumstances. By explicitly excluding attorney fees, the contract attempts to prevent a party from seeking such fees even if they would otherwise be entitled to them under Delaware law, potentially conflicting with legal precedent and the court's discretion in awarding such fees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 10 Delaware Code § 3104 (2023) - Personal jurisdiction by acts of nonresidents. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 10 - Courts and Judicial Procedure Chapter 31. PROCESS; COMMENCEMENT OF ACTIONS § 3104. Personal jurisdiction by acts of nonresidents. Universal Citation: 10 DE Code § 3104 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 3104. Personal jurisdiction by acts of nonresidents. (a) The term “person” in this section includes any natural person, association, partnership or corporation. (b) The following acts constitute legal presence within the State. Any person who commits any of the acts hereinafter enumerated thereby submits to the jurisdiction of the Delaware courts. (c) As to a cause of action brought by any person arising from any of the acts enumerated in this section, a court may exercise personal jurisdiction over any nonresident, or a personal representative, who in person or through an agent: (1) Transacts any business or performs any character of work or service in the State; (2) Contracts to supply services or things in this State; (3) Causes tortious injury in the State by an act or omission in this State; (4) Causes tortious injury in the State or outside of the State by an act or omission outside the State if the person regularly does or solicits business, engages in any other persistent course of conduct in the State or derives substantial revenue from services, or things used or consumed in the State; (5) Has an interest in, uses or possesses real property in the State; or (6) Contracts to insure or act as surety for, or on, any person, property, risk, contract, obligation or agreement located, executed or to be perform
perturbation.justification: The original text states both parties are responsible for their own fees including attorney fees. The changed text explicitly excludes attorney fees, which the perturbation explanation states may contradict statutes where the agreement is governed, regarding the right to seek attorney fees in certain breach of contract cases. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Delaware law generally allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in breach of contract cases. The exclusion attempts to prevent a party from seeking such fees. This creates a contradiction. Snippet 2 refers to jurisdiction in Delaware and does not provide information as to whether the contradiction exists or not.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties hereto irrevocably waives, and agrees not to assert, by way of motion, as a defense, counterclaim or otherwise, in any action, suit or other legal proceeding with respect to this Agreement, (a) any claim that it is not personally subject to the jurisdiction of the above-named courts for any reason, (b) that it or its property is exempt or immune from jurisdiction of any such court or from any legal process commenced in such courts (whether through service of notice, attachment before judgment, attachment in aid of execution of judgment, execution of judgment or otherwise), and (c) to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, that (i) such action, suit or other legal proceeding in any such court is brought in an inconvenient forum, (ii) the venue of such action, suit or other legal proceeding is improper or (iii) this agreement, or the subject matter hereof, may not be enforced in or by such court.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties hereto irrevocably waives, and agrees not to assert, by way of motion, as a defense, counterclaim or otherwise, in any action, suit or other legal proceeding with respect to this Agreement, (a) any claim that it is not personally subject to the jurisdiction of the above-named courts for any reason, (b) that it or its property is exempt or immune from jurisdiction of any such court or from any legal process commenced in such courts (whether through service of notice, attachment before judgment, attachment in aid of execution of judgment, execution of judgment or otherwise), and (c) that (i) such action, suit or other legal proceeding in any such court is brought in an inconvenient forum, (ii) the venue of such action, suit or other legal proceeding is improper or (iii) this agreement, or the subject matter hereof, may not be enforced in or by such court.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' from the waiver of certain defenses related to jurisdiction and forum non conveniens attempts to expand the scope of the waiver beyond what is legally permissible, potentially conflicting with Delaware law which respects limitations on waivers of rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law on the enforceability of waivers
perturbation.law_citation: IFC Credit Corp. v. Burton, 936 A.2d 777 (Del. Supr. 2007)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts typically enforce waivers of rights, but only to the extent that the waiver is knowing, voluntary, and not against public policy. By removing the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law,' the contract suggests an intent to enforce the waiver even if it is not legally permissible under Delaware law, potentially conflicting with the court's power to review and limit the scope of such waivers.

The Court has final appellate jurisdiction in criminal cases in which the sentence exceeds certain minimums, in civil cases as to final judgments and for certain other orders of the Court of Chancery, the Superior Court, and the Family Court. The Supreme Court has discretionary jurisdiction to issue writs of prohibition, quo warranto, certiorari, mandamus or to accept appeals of certain non-final orders or certified questions. Judicial Officers Hours & Locations Self-Help Guide to Appeals History Supreme Court Operating Procedures Case Scheduling FAQs Most Common Brief Deficiencies Opportunities to Serve: Boards, Commissions & Committees Opinions & Orders Rules Public Lawyer Database external link opens in new window Supreme Court News View All Press Releases View All Press Releases May 22, 2025 Delaware Supreme Court creates task force to assess alternative pathways to Delaware Bar admission The Delaware Supreme Court has authorized the creation of a task force to investigate and make recommendations about allowing alternative pathways to gain admission to the Delaware Bar beyond the Bar Exam. Creation of the task force follows through on a key recommendation from the Judicial Branch’s 2022 Strategic Plan to improve diversity in the Delaware Bench and Bar. Members of the task force, headed by Justice Gary F. Traynor, will broadly represent the state’s legal community. April 7, 2025 Delaware to Begin Using the NextGen Bar Exam in February 2028 The Delaware Supreme Court has approved the Delaware Board of Bar Examiners’ recommendation to begin administering the NextGen bar exam in February 2028. NextGen will replace the Multistate Bar Exam (“MBE”) and the Multistate Performance Tasks (“MPT”) on the Delaware bar exam. The MBE, MPT, and NextGen are developed by the National Conference of Bar Examiners (“NCBE”). NCBE has been developing NextGen since 2018 and will no longer offer the MBE and MPT after July 2028. perturbation.justification: The original text limits the waiver of rights 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law,' acknowledging legal boundaries. The changed text removes this limitation, implying an attempt to enforce the waiver even if it violates Delaware law. The Law Explanation states that Delaware courts enforce waivers only if 'knowing, voluntary, and not against public policy.' By omitting the limiting phrase, the contract contradicts this principle by suggesting the waiver applies regardless of legal permissibility, potentially conflicting with the court's oversight.
